explanation_md: The compiler folds `10 + 5` to `15`, then folds `15 * 2` to `30` at compile time under the as-if rule. The program prints `30` — the same value it would produce if the arithmetic happened at runtime.

prompt_md: Which of the following best describes the as-if rule?

options.a: The compiler must execute every line of source code in the exact order written.
options.b: The compiler may transform code in any way, as long as the observable behavior (program output, return values) is unchanged.
options.c: The compiler can only optimize code inside functions marked `const`.
options.d: The as-if rule only applies when the programmer writes `constexpr`.

explanation_md: The as-if rule permits the compiler to rearrange, remove, or precompute anything — regardless of `const` or `constexpr` keywords — as long as the externally visible behavior remains identical. Option (a) is the wrong mental model that the rule explicitly contradicts.

prompt_md: A programmer writes `int result = 60 * 60 * 24;` in their program. Under the as-if rule, what is most likely to happen at runtime?

options.a: Two multiplications are performed at runtime, just as written.
options.b: The expression is evaluated once per program run and cached.
options.c: The compiler replaces the expression with the constant `86400` and no multiplication occurs at runtime.
options.d: The result depends on whether the variable is declared `const`.

explanation_md: Constant folding is one of the most common applications of the as-if rule. The compiler can evaluate `60 * 60 * 24` at build time and embed `86400` directly into the executable, so no multiplication happens at runtime. This optimization applies regardless of `const`, though `const` does give the compiler a stronger hint.
`a - a` is always `0` regardless of the value of `a`. The compiler can fold this to the constant `0` at compile time. The program prints `0`.

options.a: The program will not compile because `area` is unused.
options.b: The program will crash at runtime because `area` is never printed.
options.c: The compiler can legally eliminate `area` entirely since it has no effect on the output, which just prints `4 5`.
options.d: The compiler is required to store `area` on the stack even if it is never used.

explanation_md: Under the as-if rule, the compiler can remove any computation that does not affect observable behavior. Since `area` is computed but never used in I/O or returned, the multiplication may be eliminated. The program compiles and prints `4 5`; option (a) is wrong because unused variables are warnings, not errors, and the code is otherwise valid.
